description:
You are an IT-support technician who has just picked up a very small ticket about a **custom per-user background service called “notifyd”**.  
The service is controlled solely by a simple text configuration file that lives inside the user’s home directory, and the on-call run-book for the service is extremely strict about how every change must be recorded.

Your goal is to:

1. Enable the service in its configuration file.  
2. Make sure a log directory exists for the service.  
3. Write a one-line change-control record in the prescribed log file.

DETAILS OF THE EXISTING SET-UP
• Configuration file:  
  /home/user/.config/notifyd/notifyd.conf  
  The file currently contains a single key–value pair on one line:  
  enabled=0

• Required log directory (may or may not exist when you start):  
  /home/user/log/notifyd/

• Required change-control log file (must end up inside the log directory above):  
  /home/user/log/notifyd/config_change.log

WHAT YOU MUST ACHIEVE
After you finish executing your commands, the system must satisfy **all** of the following conditions:

1. The file /home/user/.config/notifyd/notifyd.conf exists and contains exactly the single line:
   enabled=1

   (No extra spaces, no carriage returns, nothing else.)

2. The directory /home/user/log/notifyd/ exists (create it if it does not).  
   Make sure it is owned by the current user and has default user-writable permissions (0700 or 0755 are both fine).

3. The file /home/user/log/notifyd/config_change.log exists and its final (and only) line is exactly:
   enabled set to 1 by support

   • The line must end with a single UNIX newline “\n”.  
   • Do not place any other content in that log file; the automated test will require the file to have *only* that one line.

4. All of the above must be accomplished with a *simple* set of standard shell commands (about three or four commands in total is normal for this task).

HINTS FOR A REALISTIC APPROACH (do NOT treat these as required commands):
• `mkdir -p …` is the usual way to make sure a directory exists.  
• `echo` with redirection (`>` or `>>`) is commonly used to write configuration and log files.  
• Beware of accidentally introducing extra whitespace or quoting; the automated checker will be unforgiving.

Once you are certain the conditions are met, you are done. The automated grader will open both of the files and inspect their exact contents as described above, and it will also confirm that /home/user/log/notifyd/ really exists. No other output files or directories are required.
truth:
Initial state before the agent runs:
1. /home/user/.config/notifyd/notifyd.conf  (permissions 0644)
   Contents:
   enabled=0

2. The directory /home/user/log/notifyd/ does NOT exist.

Validation after the agent runs:
1. /home/user/.config/notifyd/notifyd.conf must exist with **exactly**:
   enabled=1\n   (one newline after the 1)

2. /home/user/log/notifyd/ must exist.

3. /home/user/log/notifyd/config_change.log must exist with **exactly**:
   enabled set to 1 by support\n   (one newline after the word “support”)

No other files are checked or required.  
The checker will confirm that:
• The config file line has no spaces other than those shown above.  
• The log directory exists and is a directory.  
• The log file has exactly one line, matching the required string verbatim.
